input_row.title: Ensure SQL server's Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) protector is encrypted with Customer-managed key
input_row.description: Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) with Customer-managed key support provides increased transparency and control over the TDE Protector, increased security with an HSM-backed external service, and promotion of separation of duties. With TDE, data is encrypted at rest with a symmetric key (called the database encryption key) stored in the database or data warehouse distribution. To protect this data encryption key (DEK) in the past, only a certificate that the Azure SQL Service managed could be used. Now, with Customer-managed key support for TDE, the DEK can be protected with an asymmetric key that is stored in the Azure Key Vault. The Azure Key Vault is a highly available and scalable cloud-based key store which offers central key management, leverages FIPS 140-2 Level 2 validated hardware security modules (HSMs), and allows separation of management of keys and data for additional security. Based on business needs or criticality of data/databases hosted on a SQL server, it is recommended that the TDE protector is encrypted by a key that is managed by the data owner (Customer-managed key).
input_row.rationale: Customer-managed key support for Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) allows user control of TDE encryption keys and restricts who can access them and when. Azure Key Vault, Azure’s cloud-based external key management system, is the first key management service where TDE has integrated support for Customer-managed keys. With Customer-managed key support, the database encryption key is protected by an asymmetric key stored in the Key Vault. The asymmetric key is set at the server level and inherited by all databases under that server. Impact: Once TDE protector is encrypted with a Customer-managed key, it transfers entire responsibility of respective key management on to you, and hence you should be more careful about doing any operations on the particular key in order to keep data from corresponding SQL server and Databases hosted accessible. When deploying Customer Managed Keys, it is prudent to ensure that you also deploy an automated toolset for managing these keys (this should include discovery and key rotation), and Keys should be stored in an HSM or hardware backed keystore, such as Azure Key Vault. As far as toolsets go, check with your cryptographic key provider, as they may well provide one as an add-on to their service.
input_row.audit: From Azure Portal 1. Go to SQL servers For the desired server instance 2. Click On Transparent data encryption 3. Ensure that Customer-managed key is selected 4. Ensure Make selected key the default TDE protector is checked From Azure CLI az account get-access-token --query "{subscripton:subscription,accessToken:accessToken}" --out tsv | xargs -L1 bash -c 'curl -X GET -H "Authorization: Bearer $1" -H "Content-Type: application/json" https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$0/resourceGroups/{resourceGroupNa me}/providers/Microsoft.Sql/servers/{serverName}/encryptionProtector?api- version=2015-05-01-preview' Ensure the output of the command contains properties kind set to azurekeyvault serverKeyType set to AzureKeyVault uri is not null From PowerShell Get-AzSqlServerTransparentDataEncryptionProtector -ServerName <ServerName> - ResourceGroupName <ResourceGroupName> Ensure the output of the command contains properties Type set to AzureKeyVault ServerKeyVaultKeyName set to KeyVaultName_KeyName_KeyIdentifierVersion KeyId set to KeyIdentifier From Azure Policy If referencing a digital copy of this Benchmark, clicking a Policy ID will open a link to the associated Policy definition in Azure. If referencing a printed copy, you can search Policy IDs from this URL: https://portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Policy/PolicyMenuBlade/~/Definitions • Policy ID: 0a370ff3-6cab-4e85-8995-295fd854c5b8 - Name: 'SQL servers should use customer-managed keys to encrypt data at rest' • Policy ID: ac01ad65-10e5-46df-bdd9-6b0cad13e1d2 - Name: 'SQL managed instances should use customer-managed keys to encrypt data at rest'
input_row.remediation: From Azure Console 1. Go to SQL servers For the desired server instance 2. Click On Transparent data encryption 3. Set Transparent data encryption to Customer-managed key 4. Browse through your key vaults to Select an existing key or create a new key in the Azure Key Vault. 5. Check Make selected key the default TDE protector From Azure CLI Use the below command to encrypt SQL server's TDE protector with a Customer- managed key az sql server tde-key set --resource-group <resourceName> --server <dbServerName> --server-key-type {AzureKeyVault} --kid <keyIdentifier> From PowerShell Use the below command to encrypt SQL server's TDE protector with a Customer- managed Key Vault key Set-AzSqlServerTransparentDataEncryptionProtector -Type AzureKeyVault -KeyId <KeyIdentifier> -ServerName <ServerName> -ResourceGroupName <ResourceGroupName> Select Y when prompted Default Value: By Default, Microsoft managed TDE protector is enabled for a SQL server. References: 1. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/relational- databases/security/encryption/transparent-data-encryption-byok-azure-sql 2. https://azure.microsoft.com/en-in/blog/preview-sql-transparent-data-encryption- tde-with-bring-your-own-key-support/ 3. https://winterdom.com/2017/09/07/azure-sql-tde-protector-keyvault 4. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/security-controls-v3- data-protection#dp-5-use-customer-managed-key-option-in-data-at-rest- encryption-when-required 5. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/key-vault/general/basic-concepts 6. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/sql/server/tde-key?view=azure-cli- latest 7. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/az.sql/get- azsqlservertransparentdataencryptionprotector?view=azps-9.2.0 8. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/az.sql/set- azsqlservertransparentdataencryptionprotector?view=azps-9.2.0 Additional Information: • This configuration is audited or can be done only on SQL server. The same configuration will be in effect on SQL Databases hosted on SQL Server. • Ensuring TDE is protected by a Customer-managed key on SQL Server does not ensure the encryption of SQL Databases. Transparent Data Encryption : Data Encryption (ON/OFF) setting on individual SQL Database decides whether database is encrypted or not.
input_row.impact: Once TDE protector is encrypted with a Customer-managed key, it transfers entire responsibility of respective key management on to you, and hence you should be more careful about doing any operations on the particular key in order to keep data from corresponding SQL server and Databases hosted accessible. When deploying Customer Managed Keys, it is prudent to ensure that you also deploy an automated toolset for managing these keys (this should include discovery and key rotation), and Keys should be stored in an HSM or hardware backed keystore, such as Azure Key Vault. As far as toolsets go, check with your cryptographic key provider, as they may well provide one as an add-on to their service.
input_row.reference: 1. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/relational- databases/security/encryption/transparent-data-encryption-byok-azure-sql 2. https://azure.microsoft.com/en-in/blog/preview-sql-transparent-data-encryption- tde-with-bring-your-own-key-support/ 3. https://winterdom.com/2017/09/07/azure-sql-tde-protector-keyvault 4. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/security-controls-v3- data-protection#dp-5-use-customer-managed-key-option-in-data-at-rest- encryption-when-required 5. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/key-vault/general/basic-concepts 6. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/sql/server/tde-key?view=azure-cli- latest 7. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/az.sql/get- azsqlservertransparentdataencryptionprotector?view=azps-9.2.0 8. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/az.sql/set- azsqlservertransparentdataencryptionprotector?view=azps-9.2.0 Additional Information: • This configuration is audited or can be done only on SQL server. The same configuration will be in effect on SQL Databases hosted on SQL Server. • Ensuring TDE is protected by a Customer-managed key on SQL Server does not ensure the encryption of SQL Databases. Transparent Data Encryption : Data Encryption (ON/OFF) setting on individual SQL Database decides whether database is encrypted or not.

first_opinion.response:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** azure_sql_server_tde_protector_customer_managed_key_verified

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:** 
The automated program would check:
- Use Azure Resource Manager APIs or Azure CLI commands to verify the SQL Server's Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) protector settings.
- Specific resources to enumerate include SQL Server instances within defined Azure subscriptions and resource groups.
- The following checks will be performed:
  - The API endpoint to retrieve the TDE protector configuration:
    ```
    GET https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/{resourceGroupName}/providers/Microsoft.Sql/servers/{serverName}/encryptionProtector?api-version=2015-05-01-preview
    ```
  - Validation criteria:
    - Check that the `kind` property is set to `azurekeyvault`.
    - Check that `serverKeyType` is set to `AzureKeyVault`.
    - Validate that the `uri` is not null.
- Pass criteria: All specifications above must be true to confirm compliance for using a Customer-managed key for TDE.
- Fail criteria: If any of the properties are incorrect or the required values are not present, the configuration will be flagged as non-compliant.

second_opinion.response:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:** The classification as Automated is correct. The use of Azure Resource Manager APIs and Azure CLI commands allows for programmatic verification of the SQL Server's TDE protector settings, making this an appropriate automated check.
- **Azure APIs/CLI Feasibility:** The assessment correctly identifies the use of Azure APIs and CLI, which are suitable for retrieving and validating the TDE protector configuration.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8

- **Automation Details:** The automation details are mostly accurate. The API endpoint is correctly specified for retrieving the TDE protector configuration. The validation criteria are appropriate for ensuring that a Customer-managed key is used.
- **Program Name Convention:** The program name follows a logical convention, although it could be slightly improved for clarity. A suggestion might be `azure_sql_tde_customer_managed_key_verification`.
- **Completeness:** The assessment covers the necessary checks for compliance, but it could include additional details on handling potential errors or exceptions in API responses.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **API Version Update:** The API version used (`2015-05-01-preview`) is outdated. It is recommended to use a more recent stable version to ensure compatibility and access to the latest features.
- **Error Handling:** The assessment should include considerations for handling API errors, such as network issues or permission errors, which could affect the automation's reliability.
- **Additional Checks:** Consider verifying the Azure Key Vault configuration to ensure that the key used is properly managed and secured, including access policies and key rotation practices.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment demonstrates a good understanding of Azure capabilities and security best practices. The use of Azure APIs and CLI for automation is well-founded, and the validation criteria are appropriate for the control in question.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** Modify

- **Modify Details:** Update the API version to a more recent stable version to ensure the automation remains effective and compatible with current Azure services. Additionally, include error handling procedures and consider additional checks related to the Azure Key Vault configuration for comprehensive compliance verification.

final_decision.response:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:** Both AI opinions agree that the compliance control can be verified using Azure Resource Manager APIs and Azure CLI commands, which are capable of programmatically checking the SQL Server's TDE protector settings. The necessary properties, such as `kind` and `serverKeyType`, are exposed through standard Azure resource APIs, making it feasible to automate this check without requiring manual intervention.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
azure_sql_server_tde_customer_managed_key_verification

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **Azure Resource/Service:** SQL Servers
- **API/CLI Method:** Use Azure Resource Manager API endpoint `GET https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/{resourceGroupName}/providers/Microsoft.Sql/servers/{serverName}/encryptionProtector?api-version=2015-05-01-preview`
- **Properties to Check:** 
  - `kind` property should be set to `azurekeyvault`
  - `serverKeyType` should be `AzureKeyVault`
  - `uri` should not be null
- **Pass Criteria:** All specified properties must meet the expected values indicating that a Customer-managed key is used.
- **Fail Criteria:** Any deviation from the expected property values indicates non-compliance.

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:** There were no disagreements between the AI opinions; both supported the automated approach. The decision was made based on the capabilities of Azure APIs to perform the necessary checks programmatically.
